content.full_text:
06-11-90 03:01PM FROM UCLA LAW WORD PROC P01

UCLA Law School
405 Hilgard
Los Angeles, California 90024

June 11, 1990

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
928 J Street, Suite 800
Box 867
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin,

This letter is a request for formal written advice within the meaning of Government Code §11342 (b) and Prior Regulation § 2039. The request is made in behalf of Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc., 291 South La Cienega, Suite 200, Beverly Hills, California 90211. The undersigned, who serves as counsel to Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc., has been authorized by Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc. to submit this request.

Although the questions are stated in generic form, they are based on specific factual scenarios involving Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc., which serves as consultant to a leading publisher of state mail, manages election campaigns for federal, state and local office, and provides political consulting services to elected officeholders. The situations described in questions A through C involve general election activities of Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc. and in future elections, as is evidenced by the fact that they have arisen routinely and very commonly in the past.

Each of the following situations, A and C are assumed to be candidates running for state or local office. In addition, C is an incumbent state or local officeholder. (It is in the staffs' view that D's questions other than C is running for reelection or running for a different office, you are requested to give written regarding each situation. A is assumed to be a committee supporting or opposing a state or local ballot measure. C does not "control" either A's campaign committee or E within the meaning of Government Code § 82016.

The questions involve the sending of endorsement messages to voters. It is assumed that the voters who receive the messages reside in the jurisdictions within which A and C are candidates and within which the ballot measure supported or opposed by B is being voted upon.

JUN 11 '90 15:05         213 206 7010  PAGE 001

- 2 -

In each case it is assumed that C supports and is willing to
endorse the candidacy of A or the ballot measure position favored
by B. A's or B's agents consult and cooperate with C to the
extent of drafting a message to disseminate the endorsement,
working with C on the content of the endorsement message to
assure that it will have maximum political effect-
iveness in support of A or the position favored by B, and, where
applicable, in obtaining C's letterhead, signature, appearance on
film or tape, and the like.

Payment for dissemination of the endorsement message and
decisions regarding the means, extent, and timing of the dis-
semination are made by A or B, not by C. It is possible,
however, that A may make suggestions regarding these decisions.

In each case, the endorsement message expressly urges a vote
for A or for B's favored position, but not for C,
identified by name and by the elective office he or she currently
holds or is in position to C's current candidacy. The
overall thrust and emphasis of the message relates to the reasons
for voting for A or for B's favored position.

In each case, the question whether a "contribution" has been
made is analyzed with respect to the provisions of Prop. 73
regarding the size of contributions and prohibiting
transfers.

The questions are as follows:

1. If A pays for slate mail advertising containing C's mes-
sage of endorsement of A, has A made a contribution to C?

2. If B pays for slate mail advertising containing C's mes-
sage endorsing B's ballot measure position, has B made a con-
tribution to C?

3. If A pays for a mass mailing consisting of a letter on C's
letterhead and over C's signature, the content of which urges the
recipient to vote for A, has A made a contribution to C?

4. If B pays for a mass mailing consisting of a letter on C's
letterhead and over C's signature, the content of which urges the
recipient to vote for A's favored position on the ballot measure,
has B made a contribution to C?

5. If A pays for a television advertisement in which C is
seen and heard urging support for A's candidacy, has A made a
contribution to C?

6. If B pays for a television advertisement in which C is
seen and heard urging support for B's position on the ballot
measure, has B made a contribution to C?

JUN 11 '90 15:06         213 206 7010  PAGE.002

This image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with a cream/off-white colored background. There is no visible text or other content to transcribe.

06-11-90  03:15PM FROM UCLA LAW WORD PROC                                F03


                                  - 3 -


7. In questions 5 and 6, is the answer affected by whether
C's appearance makes up a small (e.g. 7 seconds in a 30-second
advertisement) or large (e.g. 25 seconds in a 30-second adver-
tisement) percentage of the entire advertisement?


                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]
                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Attorney for
                                        D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc.




JUN 11 '90 15:06                        213 206 7010   PAGE.003

The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with very faint markings. There is no clearly legible text visible to transcribe.

sections.question:
1. If A pays for slate mail advertising containing C's message of endorsement of A, has A made a contribution to C?

2. If B pays for slate mail advertising containing C's message endorsing B's ballot measure position, has B made a contribution to C?

3. If A pays for a mass mailing consisting of a letter on C's letterhead and over C's signature, the content of which urges the recipient to vote for A, has A made a contribution to C?

4. If B pays for a mass mailing consisting of a letter on C's letterhead and over C's signature, the content of which urges the recipient to vote for B's favored position on the ballot measure, has B made a contribution to C?

5. If A pays for a television advertisement in which C is seen and heard urging support for A's candidacy, has A made a contribution to C?

6. If B pays for a television advertisement in which C is seen and heard urging support for B's position on the ballot measure, has B made a contribution to C?

7. In questions 5 and 6, is the answer affected by whether C's appearance makes up a small (e.g., 7 seconds in a 30-second advertisement) or large (e.g., 25 seconds in a 30-second advertisement) percentage of the entire advertisement?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether payments by candidates or ballot measure committees for dissemination of endorsement messages featuring incumbent officeholders' names, letterhead, signatures, or appearances constitute contributions under Proposition 73's contribution limitations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Incoming request letter, not FPPC response. Seven distinct questions regarding contribution definitions. Document includes detailed factual assumptions about candidate relationships, endorsement content, and payment arrangements. Second image page appears blank/overexposed with no recoverable content.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. If A pays for slate mail advertising containing C's message of endorsement of A, has A made a contribution to C?

2. If B pays for slate mail advertising containing C's message endorsing B's ballot measure position, has B made a contribution to C?

3. If A pays for a mass mailing consisting of a letter on C's letterhead and over C's signature, the content of which urges the recipient to vote for A, has A made a contribution to C?

4. If B pays for a mass mailing consisting of a letter on C's letterhead and over C's signature, the content of which urges the recipient to vote for B's favored position on the ballot measure, has B made a contribution to C?

5. If A pays for a television advertisement in which C is seen and heard urging support for A's candidacy, has A made a contribution to C?

6. If B pays for a television advertisement in which C is seen and heard urging support for B's position on the ballot measure, has B made a contribution to C?

7. In questions 5 and 6, is the answer affected by whether C's appearance makes up a small (e.g., 7 seconds in a 30-second advertisement) or large (e.g., 25 seconds in a 30-second advertisement) percentage of the entire advertisement?
embedding.first_500_words: UCLA Law School 405 Hilgard Los Angeles, California 90024 June 11, 1990 Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804-0807 Dear Mr. Hallabrin, This letter is a request for formal written advice within the meaning of Government Code § 83114 (b) and FPPC Regulation 28239 (b). The request is made in behalf of Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc., 291 South La Cienega, Suite 200, Beverly Hills, California 90211. The undersigned, who serves as counsel to Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc., has been authorized by Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc. to submit this request. Although the questions are stated in generic form, they are not by any means hypothetical. Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc. serves as consultant to a leading publisher of slate mail, manages election campaigns for federal, state, and local office and for or against ballot measures, and provides political consulting services to elected officeholders. The situations described in the questions are likely to arise in this year's general election activities of Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc., and in future elections, as is evidenced by the fact that they have arisen routinely and very commonly in the past. In each of the following situations, A and C are assumed to be candidates running for state or local office. In addition, C is an incumbent state or local officeholder. (If in the staff's opinion the answers to the questions are affected by whether C is running for reelection or running for a different office, you are requested to give advice regarding each situation.) B is assumed to be a committee supporting or opposing a state or local ballot measure. Q does not "control" either A's campaign committee or B within the meaning of Government Code § 82016. The questions involve the sending of endorsement messages to voters. It is assumed that the voters who receive the messages reside in the jurisdictions within which A and C are candidates and within which the ballot measure supported or opposed by B is being voted upon. In each case it is assumed that C supports and is willing to endorse the candidacy of A or the ballot measure position favored by B. A's or B's agents consult and cooperate with C to the extent of obtaining C's consent to distributing the endorsement, working with C on the content of the endorsement message to assure that it is acceptable to C and of maximum political effectiveness in support of A or the position favored by B, and, where applicable, in obtaining C's letterhead, signature, appearance on film or tape, and the like. Payment for dissemination of the endorsement message and decisions regarding the means, extent, and timing of the dissemination are made by A or B, not by C. It is possible, however, that C may make suggestions regarding these decisions. In each case, the endorsement message expressly urges a vote for A or for B's favored position, but not for C. C
embedding.summary: Berman & D'Agostino Campaigns, Inc. seeks formal written FPPC advice regarding whether payments for various endorsement message formats (slate mail, letters, television advertisements) featuring incumbent officeholders constitute reportable contributions under Proposition 73.
